s1.subline: For founder-led service businesses that close through sales calls. In 45 days, on LinkedIn.

s2.headline: "I need to think about it" shows up in many ways.

quotes.q: "Can you send me a proposal so I'll get *right* back to you."
"Let me discuss this with my partner."
quotes.a: But you don't hear from them.

quotes.q: "Sounds good but the timing isn't right."

quotes.a: And it's Q1 next year, and still circling.

s3.headline: The sale broke in 3 places, and you kinda know.

rows.q: "You're for everyone. So the right person can't find you."
a: You take every kind of client because saying no feels like leaving money on the table.
Then, your offers got broader because of the clients you worked with, not the ones you could best serve.
Finally... The right person arrives, scans what you do, sees everything and nothing, and leaves.

rows.q: "The site lists every service you've ever sold. Your ideal buyer needs only 1."
a: You keep every service on the menu because removing one feels like closing a revenue door.
But your client needs to feel you are the expert on their specific problem.
When they scan your offer, they can't find what they came to solve.
It reads like a restaurant menu. Complete from your side.
Unclear from theirs.

rows.q: "They seemed convinced on the call. Then... silence."
a: You felt good after the call. They asked smart questions and said they liked what you do.
But after you closed Google Meet the conversation kept going. With their partner. With their pillow. With their team. With whoever they trust to tell them whether this is worth it.

You don't get to be in that conversation. You don't get to hear what they said about your price, your competition, or how they remembered you 30 minutes after the call. You just get the email three weeks later. "It's good, but it's not the right time."
Next call, the same thing. Nothing changed.

s4.headline: This is how we fix those 3.

bodies: Instead of selling many things to many people, we narrow the offer.
We rebuild it around the clients who paid on time and referred others. The ones whose results you're proud to show.
Then your offer learns from every call. The ones that close show what's working. The ones that don't show where the offer still bends.
We reflect the offer across everything a prospect reads before the call.
Site, LinkedIn, content. Each surface says the same thing about the same problem for the same person.

Then your language learns from every call. The lines that made prospects sit up. The lines they ignored. By week three, the surfaces are written from real conversations, not from a strategy doc on day one.
The prospect arrives on the call already knowing who you help and what you do.
The call doesn't restart the pitch from zero. It moves to fit, scope, and the specific shape of their problem.

Then your call learns from the last one. The objection that showed up twice becomes content that handles it before the next call. The pricing question that derailed the conversation becomes part of the offer page.

closer: By day 45, the calls aren't the same calls. Not the offer. Not the website.
Every reply taught the next post. Every call taught the next offer. By the end, nothing is what it was at the start.

s6.headline: Nothing in the pipeline moves on its own.
s6.payoff: Every sales call rewrites what came before it.

s7.headline: Forty-five days. Two phases.
s7.phase1Heading: The first three weeks build the engine.
s7.phase1Body: Week 1, what you offer. Week 2, what you say. Week 3, what goes live, and how you sell it.
s7.phase2Heading: The next three weeks run the engine.
s7.phase2Body: Replies come in. Calls happen. Every one of them updates the offer, the content, and the next batch of outreach.
s7.closingLine1: You bring the current state. Site, offer, outreach, call recordings.
s7.closingLine2: We rebuild the parts making the deal harder than it needs to be.

s8.founderHeadline: I spent ten years doing this at Fortune 500 companies.
s8.founderBody1: Global CRM and customer journeys at adidas. User journey expert at Heineken. $80M enterprise sales at DELL.

faq.q: Your case study is a coach. Why should I believe this works for my business?
a: A career coach is the proof the mechanism runs end-to-end. Six booked calls in 21 days from zero LinkedIn pipeline. 28% reply rate on warm outreach. Connection acceptance from 5% to 30%. The loop closed.

What every version of the sprint inherits from that: positioning built from real client data, not personas. Content from lived moments, not tips. Outreach run by a trained operator, not the founder. Sales calls feeding back into the work while the sprint is still live.

What changes by business type: the sprint length, the outreach volume, the shape of the offer. A coach selling 1:1 packages and a consultancy selling a six-month engagement need different versions of the same machine.

Before building Journify I spent ten years on the other side of the table. Buying from agencies at adidas, Heineken, and DELL. Working with iProspect, Wunderman, and others. I know what gets a deal approved internally and what gets it dismissed after a good call.

faq.q: I've tried LinkedIn outreach before. Got nothing. Why would this be different?
a: Because the outreach isn't the problem.
When LinkedIn doesn't work, it's usually one of three things. The profile doesn't hold up when a prospect checks it. The DM reads like a template. Or the list is wrong.
Most founders fix the DM. That doesn't fix anything if the profile and the list are still off.

The sprint starts with the offer. One expensive problem, one person it's for. Everything else gets built from there. The Sales Navigator filters, the messaging, the profile, all of it runs from that foundation. The right person receives a message, checks the profile, sees something that reads like their exact situation, and replies.
LinkedIn works when the foundation underneath it is right. Most of the time, it isn't.

faq.q: Who actually runs the outreach? How do I know my LinkedIn reputation is safe?
a: I run it on your account, with your credentials. Not from a Journify account. Not from a VA's profile. Yours.

Before anything goes out, the profile is rebuilt and the messaging is tested. The sequences, the Sales Navigator filters, the reply handling, all of that is managed by a trained operator working under my review. You see what goes out. Replies come to you.
Your reputation stays visible the whole time. Nothing happens in a black box.

faq.q: What do I actually keep after 45 days if I don't continue?
a: Everything from the sprint is yours.

The booked calls and new leads, first. Then the rewritten offer page. The rewritten LinkedIn profile. The content strategy. The sales call language. The outreach SOPs. The trained operator and the onboarding package to keep them running independently.

And the sales call learning mechanism. The part that updates the offer, the openers, and the next batch of outreach from real conversations. That's the piece you can keep applying long after the 45 days end.

What stops when the sprint ends is the outreach execution. The work doesn't run itself. If you want it to keep moving past day 45, there's a retainer. If you don't, you have a documented system you can hand to whoever picks it up next.

s8.payoff: If you've read this far, the next move is a fifteen-minute call.
